title: Retail investing: Gen Z backs ETFs as AI drives frenzy
full_text:
Katie Last was listening to a podcast while driving through the suburbs of Melbourne for a work trip when a line grabbed her attention and kick-started the 27-year-old's journey into the topsy-turvy world of shares.
"The best time to invest was yesterday. The second-best time to invest is today," a finance influencer told listeners, borrowing a line commonly attributed to Berkshire Hathaway legend Warren Buffett.
So in June 2021, as her hometown was still going through a brutal pattern of coming in and out of COVID-19 lockdowns, the then-22-year-old created an account on Spaceship - a technology-focused investment platform that was at the time backed by Mike Cannon-Brookes.
Last, who now works in marketing for Air Asia, made an initial investment of $5000 on the platform. And she soon tipped the same amount into a high-growth ETF, or exchange-traded fund, with Vanguard.
In doing so, Last joined a wave of Gen Zs who, with one eye on the daunting prospect of breaking into Australia's housing market, have decided to take the bold leap into retail trading, amid a broader surge in its popularity.
Retail investors have been feasting on the artificial intelligence trade since the public launch of OpenAI's ChatGPT in November 2022. But their growing participation was underscored last month by SpaceX's record-breaking float that targeted individual investors via brokerages such as CommSec with a significant 20 per cent allocation.
More than 37,000 customers opened international trading accounts on CommSec in just three weeks, equating to two-thirds of its annual run rate. About 28,000 customers followed through and bid for SpaceX shares, raising an estimated $1 billion - four times the number of orders CommSec attracted for its most popular Australian listing.
However, this growth in non-professional participation is not without risk. Indeed, the Bank of International Settlements, often regarded as the central bank for central banks, this week warned that the surging number of retail investors has heightened the risk of a much bigger economic fallout from a sharemarket correction.
Nevertheless, the growth in young retail traders has led to a rocketing number of ETFs, which offer an easy, low-stress way for everyday people to invest their money in the market.
Those funds are offered by firms such as Vanguard, Betashares and Global X. Data released by the ASX this week showed that 72 new ETFs hit the boards in the 2026 financial year, which was the highest on record, and a jump from 50 in 2025.
There are now 458 ETFs on the Australian bourse, according to the ASX, compared to about 200 in 2020 and just 19 in late 2008, with a large chunk of growth coming from Gen Z, which broadly includes teens to 30-year-olds.
But while it is clear young people are piling into retail investing with an enthusiasm perhaps only rivalled by their hunger for TikTok, vapes and frozen yoghurt, they are not all investing in the same ways.
In fact, a classic gender divide may be emerging, with internal data released by retail brokerage giant CommSec, which is owned by Commonwealth Bank and used by about 40 per cent of retail investors, showing that men prefer buying individual stocks and women opt for ETFs. Last quipped that she and her partner, Charlie, who likes hunting for small caps in the more high-risk, high-reward end of the market, fit the bill perfectly.
But another young female investor, Suraksha Guragain, said that gender has nothing to do with her investment strategy. The 27-year-old, who has lived in Sydney for three years after moving from Nepal to complete an information technology master's degree, reinvests 30 per cent of her monthly income as a consultant.
Like Last, she invests in two ETFs, also through Vanguard. But Guragain said that she chose this pathway over investing directly in specific equities (she had considered Apple) because she has no financial safety net in Australia, and cannot afford to take a hit.
"I don't invest in ETFs because I'm a woman. I do it because I cannot take those kinds of risks."
Deakin Kuruwita, a first-year commerce student at Monash University in Melbourne, is another young investor. The 18-year-old was inspired to get into trading after reading Psychology of Money, a bestseller that explores how emotions influence financial decisions.
Kuruwita already has about $13,000 invested in the sharemarket, split evenly between ETFs and ASX small caps.
Over in Singapore, the aim for Brisbane-born-and-bred Jacob Pratt, a 27-year-old who works as an e-commerce lead for cosmetics giant L'Oreal, is ultimately safety and financial freedom.
Pratt, who now has about $150,000 invested across various sharemarkets, including in Australia and Hong Kong, got started early in the game after reading The Barefoot Investor and is bullish on ASX small-cap gold explorers.
Fairfax Media Management Pty Limited
author: Gus McCubbing
heading: Data Science and AI
